questions.question: How do protein and exercise work synergistically for mental health?

options.text: Protein has no connection to exercise or mental health

Protein provides amino acids (tryptophan → serotonin, tyrosine → dopamine/norepinephrine) — the same neurotransmitters that exercise upregulates; adequate protein supports both recovery and mood chemistry
Only carbohydrates matter for mental health
Protein is only for bodybuilders, not mental health

questions.explanation: Protein provides the building blocks for neurotransmitter synthesis: tryptophan (from protein) → serotonin, and tyrosine (from protein) → dopamine and norepinephrine. These are the same neurotransmitters that exercise upregulates. Adequate protein (0.8-2.2g/kg depending on activity level) supports muscle recovery (enabling consistent exercise) AND provides raw materials for the mood chemistry changes exercise triggers. Post-exercise protein (20-30g within 2 hours) optimizes both.

questions.question: What is the relationship between carbohydrates and serotonin synthesis?

options.text: Carbohydrates have no effect on serotonin
Insulin released after carb intake helps tryptophan cross the blood-brain barrier, increasing serotonin synthesis; carbs also replenish glycogen post-exercise (low glycogen = poor mood, fatigue)
All carbohydrates are harmful for mental health
Only ketogenic diets support mental health

questions.explanation: Carbohydrates trigger insulin release, which helps tryptophan (serotonin precursor) cross the blood-brain barrier more effectively. Additionally, carbs replenish glycogen (stored energy) after exercise — low glycogen is associated with irritability, fatigue, and poor mood. The lesson notes that chronic low-carb diets can impair mood in some people, especially with high exercise volumes. Complex carbs (whole grains, oats, sweet potatoes, fruit) are preferred over refined sugars.

questions.question: Why are omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA) particularly important for the exercise-mental health connection?

options.text: Omega-3s have no relevance to mental health or exercise

Omega-3s reduce inflammation and enhance BDNF signaling — complementing exercise's BDNF boost and anti-inflammatory effects; meta-analyses show significant depression reduction with EPA-rich supplementation
Only saturated fats matter for brain health
Omega-3s are harmful to mental health

questions.explanation: Omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA from fatty fish, algae supplements) have anti-inflammatory effects and enhance BDNF signaling. This synergizes with exercise's BDNF boost and anti-inflammatory benefits. Meta-analyses show omega-3 supplementation (especially EPA-rich) significantly reduces depressive symptoms. The Mediterranean diet (rich in fish, olive oil, nuts) is associated with 30% lower depression risk. Recommended: fatty fish 2-3x/week or algae-based omega-3 if plant-based.

questions.question: What is the recommended post-exercise nutrition timing and macronutrient ratio?

options.text: Post-exercise nutrition doesn't matter at all
Within 2 hours post-exercise: carbs + protein in a 3:1 or 4:1 ratio (e.g., Greek yogurt + fruit, chicken + rice) to replenish glycogen, support muscle repair, and optimize neurotransmitter synthesis
You should fast for 24 hours after exercise
Only protein matters post-exercise; never eat carbs

questions.explanation: Post-exercise nutrition (within 2 hours): carbs + protein in 3:1 or 4:1 ratio. Carbs replenish glycogen (preventing mood crashes and fatigue). Protein supports muscle repair and provides amino acids for serotonin/dopamine synthesis. Examples: Greek yogurt + fruit, chicken + rice + vegetables, protein smoothie + banana. This timing and ratio optimize both physical recovery and mental health benefits by supporting the neurobiological changes exercise triggers.

questions.question: How would you describe your current nutrition habits? Are there any patterns you've noticed between what you eat and how you feel during/after exercise? What's one small nutrition change you could make to better support your movement and mental health?
questions.explanation: This reflection helps you identify nutrition-exercise-mood connections in your own experience and choose one actionable change. Common patterns: skipping pre-exercise meals → low energy, irritability during workout. Post-exercise junk food → mood crash later. Chronic low-carb + high exercise → brain fog, mood instability. Inadequate protein → poor recovery, fatigue. One small change examples: add pre-exercise banana + nut butter, include protein at each meal, eat within 2 hrs post-exercise, add fatty fish 2x/week for omega-3s.
